Baby Naming Trends in the United States

A Data Analytics Report for the New York Toy Design Agency

June 18, 2026

1 Executive Summary

This report analyses US baby naming trends from 1910 to 2014 using Social Security Administration records covering over 5.6 million name-year-state observations. The analysis was supplemented with Billboard Hot 100 chart data (1958вҖ“2021) and HBO titles and credits data to identify cultural drivers of naming spikes.

Key findings:

  • Popular baby names were highly persistent before 1990, particularly for boys. Since the 1990s, name persistence has declined significantly for both genders, confirming the agencyвҖҷs hypothesis.
  • GirlsвҖҷ names have historically been more susceptible to cultural influence than boysвҖҷ names.
  • Specific cultural events вҖ” including TV show characters, music artists, and movies вҖ” demonstrably cause sharp spikes in baby name popularity within 1вҖ“2 years of the event.
  • Name trends have fragmented in recent decades, with no single name dominating across the 2000s and 2010s the way names like Michael, James, and Mary did in mid-century decades.

2 Data Overview

Dataset Source Period Size
Baby Names by US State Social Security Administration 1910вҖ“2014 5.6M rows
Billboard Hot 100 Billboard Magazine 1958вҖ“2021 330K rows
HBO Titles IMDb / HBO 1901вҖ“2022 3,294 titles
HBO Credits IMDb / HBO 1901вҖ“2022 66,393 records

Note: Baby name data was aggregated nationally by summing counts across all 50 US states to enable meaningful national ranking comparisons.

3 Name Persistence Over Time

3.1 Methodology

To measure whether popular names persist over time, Spearman rank correlation was computed between each yearвҖҷs top 25 most popular names and the rankings of those same names in the following 1, 2, and 3 years. A correlation close to 1.0 indicates that the same names remained popular; a correlation closer to 0 indicates rapid turnover in naming preferences.

Why Spearman rank correlation? Unlike Pearson correlation, SpearmanвҖҷs method is based on ranks rather than raw counts, making it robust to the large variation in absolute name counts across decades (e.g.В the baby boom years had far more births than the 1910s).

3.2 Results

Spearman rank correlation between each yearвҖҷs top 25 names and the next 1вҖ“3 years. Red dotted line marks 1990.

Key observations:

  • Pre-1990: Both boys and girls names showed high persistence (correlation > 0.90), meaning the same popular names recurred year after year. Boys names were particularly stable, often exceeding 0.95.
  • Post-1990: Correlations became more volatile and trended lower, especially at the 3-year lag. This confirms the agencyвҖҷs suspicion that naming trends have become less persistent since the 1990s.
  • Girls names: Showed a notable dip in the mid-1960s to 1970s вҖ” coinciding with the cultural revolution, second-wave feminism, and the explosion of pop culture through television. Girls names recovered in the 1980s before declining again post-1990.
  • Boys names: Remained stable longer but showed a sharp drop in persistence after 1990, with the 3-year lag correlation falling below 0.60 at times.

Implication for the toy agency: Toy character names chosen today are less likely to remain culturally relevant 3 years from now compared to pre-1990. The agency should plan for more frequent name refreshes in their product lines.

4 Name Popularity by Decade

4.1 Boys Names

Top 5 boys baby names per decade. Bubble size and colour indicate total count of babies named.

Key observations:

  • Michael, John, James, and Robert dominated from the 1940s through the 1980s вҖ” these names achieved counts exceeding 800,000 per decade (dark brown bubbles), making them the most dominant names in US history.
  • Mid-century dominance: The 1950s and 1960s saw the highest name concentration вҖ” a small number of names accounted for a very large share of all boys born.
  • Fragmentation post-1990: From the 1990s onward, newer names like Jacob, Joshua, Matthew, and Daniel appear at 200,000вҖ“400,000 levels, indicating that no single name dominates the way Michael once did.
  • 2010s: Ethan, Noah, and Mason appear with small grey bubbles (below 200,000), confirming the fragmentation of naming preferences in the most recent decade.

4.2 Girls Names

Top 5 girls baby names per decade. Bubble size and colour indicate total count of babies named.

Key observations:

  • Mary is the most dominant girls name in US history вҖ” commanding 600,000+ counts from the 1910s through the 1950s (red and dark red bubbles).
  • Jennifer and Linda rose to dominance in the 1960sвҖ“1980s before fading entirely from the top 5 by the 2000s вҖ” a clear example of a name fad that peaked and faded.
  • Girls names fragment earlier: Unlike boys names which remained stable until the 1990s, girls names show fragmentation starting from the 1970s, consistent with the Spearman plot findings.
  • 2000sвҖ“2010s: Emma, Olivia, Isabella, Sophia, and Ava all appear with small grey/blue bubbles, confirming that modern girls naming is highly dispersed with no dominant name.

5 Culturally Influenced Name Spikes

5.1 Methodology

Year-on-year percentage changes in national name counts were computed for all names. Names were flagged as spikes when: (1) their count grew by more than 1,000% in a single year, and (2) the name had at least 500 total counts in the spike year (to exclude obscure names). Spike years were then cross-referenced with Billboard chart data and known cultural events.

5.2 Spike Detection Results

The table below summarises the most dramatic name spikes detected:

Name Spike Year Previous Count Spike Count % Change Likely Cause
Aaliyah 1994 5 1,412 28,140% R&B artist AaliyahвҖҷs debut album
Mallory 1983 5 658 13,060% Family Ties TV show character
Cataleya 2012 8 616 7,600% Colombiana movie (2011)
Katina 1972 48 2,726 5,579% Where the Heart Is TV show
Shania 1995 13 546 4,100% Shania TwainвҖҷs rising fame
Whitney 1985 вҖ“ 9,400+ вҖ“ Whitney HoustonвҖҷs debut album

5.3 Cultural Spike Visualisation

Baby name counts over time for culturally influenced names. Dashed lines mark the cultural event year.

Name-by-name analysis:

  • Aaliyah (1994): Billboard data confirms Aaliyah charted at rank 5 in 1994 with вҖңBack & ForthвҖқ. The name went from near-zero to 1,412 babies in a single year вҖ” the clearest example of a music artist driving a naming spike in this dataset.
  • Whitney (1985): Whitney HoustonвҖҷs debut album in 1985 triggered a massive and sustained spike reaching nearly 10,000 babies per year at its peak. The name persisted for over a decade before fading вҖ” suggesting sustained cultural influence rather than a short fad.
  • Mallory (1983): Family Ties premiered on NBC in 1982 featuring the character Mallory Keaton. The name spiked sharply in 1983 and maintained elevated counts through the 1990s before declining. Note: the HBO dataset does not cover NBC shows, but the timing provides strong circumstantial evidence.
  • Katina (1972): Confirmed by the exam brief as a character on the TV show вҖңWhere the Heart IsвҖқ (1972). The spike is sharp and short-lived вҖ” typical of a one-season character rather than a long-running show.
  • Shania (1995): Shania TwainвҖҷs вҖңAny Man of MineвҖқ charted on Billboard in 1995, triggering a naming spike. Her later success in 1997 caused a secondary surge, visible in the plot as a double peak.
  • Cataleya (2012): The movie Colombiana (2011) featured a lead character named Cataleya. The name barely existed before 2011 and spiked to over 600 babies in 2012 вҖ” a clear movie-driven naming event.

5.4 Billboard Confirmation

Cross-referencing with Billboard Hot 100 data confirmed the following:

Name Billboard Evidence
Aaliyah Charted at rank 5 in 1994 with вҖңBack & ForthвҖқ вҖ” direct match
Shania Charted in 1995 and peaked in 1997 with Shania Twain вҖ” partial match
Whitney Pre-dates Billboard analysis window but consistent with HoustonвҖҷs 1985 debut

Limitation: The HBO credits database covers only HBO-distributed content and does not include network TV shows (NBC, CBS, ABC). Several spikes attributed to TV characters (Mallory, Katina) could not be confirmed through HBO data alone. A broader entertainment database would strengthen these findings.

6 Conclusions and Recommendations

6.1 Key Conclusions

  1. Name persistence has declined since the 1990s. Popular names today are significantly less likely to remain popular 3 years later compared to pre-1990 trends. This is true for both boys and girls, though girls names have historically been more volatile.

  2. Mid-century names dominated at scale never seen since. Names like Michael, Mary, James, and John achieved counts exceeding 800,000 per decade. Modern naming is far more fragmented, with no single name commanding comparable dominance.

  3. Cultural events reliably cause naming spikes. Music artists, TV characters, and movie characters demonstrably drive sharp increases in name popularity within 1вҖ“2 years of their cultural peak. The effect is strongest for music artists (Whitney, Aaliyah) and sustained TV shows (Mallory/Family Ties).

  4. Girls names respond more quickly to cultural trends than boys names, which historically showed greater inertia.

6.2 Recommendations for the Toy Design Agency

  • Avoid names with long histories of dominance (Michael, Mary, Jennifer) вҖ” these have already peaked and are declining in cultural relevance.
  • Monitor emerging music and streaming content вҖ” the clearest predictor of a naming spike is a prominent artist or character debut. Names associated with new Netflix/HBO releases or chart-topping artists are likely candidates for the next spike.
  • Plan for shorter product cycles вҖ” given the post-1990 decline in name persistence, toy character names should be refreshed every 3вҖ“5 years rather than relying on decade-long dominance.
  • Gender-specific strategies вҖ” boys toy names can afford slightly longer product cycles given their historically greater naming persistence. Girls toy names should be updated more frequently.
  • Target the 25вҖ“40 name range вҖ” names that are popular but not yet dominant (200,000вҖ“400,000 per decade range) are at the growth phase of their cycle and represent the best opportunity for the agency.

Data source: US Social Security Administration baby name records (March 2015). Billboard Hot 100 data sourced from Billboard Magazine. HBO titles and credits data sourced from IMDb.
